studyMetadata.title: Exploring Hebrews
studyMetadata.subtitle: Bible Study
studyMetadata.description: The letter to the Hebrews makes one enormous claim and presses it from every angle: Jesus is better. Better than the prophets, better than the angels, better than Moses, better than the Levitical priesthood, better than the old covenant and all its sacrifices. It is a book written for people who are tempted to drift — to let the extraordinary become ordinary, to trade the reality for the shadow, to give up on Christ because the cost of following him has become too high. Its message is as urgent now as it was for its first readers: do not ignore so great a salvation.
studyMetadata.author: Julie Rowbory

theme.theme-annotation: Hebrews study theme — deep parchment and iron palette. Weighty, ancient, and anchored, reflecting themes of Christ's supremacy, covenant sacrifice, and perseverance.

elements.text: The letter to the Hebrews opens with one of the most compressed and extraordinary Christological statements in the entire New Testament. In two verses, the writer describes a God who has been speaking throughout history — and who has now spoken his final, definitive word in his Son. Everything that follows in the letter — the priesthood, the sacrifice, the new covenant, the warnings against drift — rests on what is established here: who Jesus is, and what that means for how seriously we must take what he has said.

elements.question: Read the passage for this session before you begin…
elements.answer:
1:1After God spoke long ago in various portions and in various ways to our ancestors through the prophets, 2in these last days he has spoken to us in a Son, whom he appointed heir of all things, and through whom he created the world. 3The Son is the radiance of his glory and the representation of his essence, and he sustains all things by his powerful word, and so when he had accomplished cleansing for sins, he sat down at the right hand of the Majesty on high. 4Thus he became so far superior to the angels as he has inherited a name superior to theirs.

5For to which of the angels did God ever say, "You are my Son! Today I have fathered you"? And in another place, "I will be his father and he will be my son"? 6But when he again brings his firstborn into the world, he says, "Let all the angels of God worship him!" 7And he says of the angels, "He makes his angels spirits and his ministers a flame of fire," 8but of the Son he says, "Your throne, O God, is forever and ever, and a righteous scepter is the scepter of your kingdom. 9You have loved righteousness and hated lawlessness. So God, your God, has anointed you over your companions with the oil of rejoicing." 10And, "You founded the earth in the beginning, Lord, and the heavens are the works of your hands. 11They will perish, but you continue. And they will all grow old like a garment, 12and like a robe you will fold them up and like a garment they will be changed, but you are the same and your years will never run out." 13But to which of the angels has he ever said, "Sit at my right hand until I make your enemies a footstool for your feet"? 14Are they not all ministering spirits, sent out to serve those who will inherit salvation?

2:1Therefore we must pay closer attention to what we have heard, so that we do not drift away. 2For if the message spoken through angels proved to be so firm that every violation and disobedience received its just penalty, 3how will we escape if we neglect such a great salvation? It was first communicated through the Lord and was confirmed to us by those who heard him, 4while God confirmed their witness with signs and wonders and various miracles and gifts of the Holy Spirit distributed according to his will.

elements.text: As you read, think about the overall impression the writer creates of Jesus in these opening verses, and the warning he draws from it at the end.

elements.question: What words or phrases from the passage stay with you most after a first reading?
What contrast does the writer draw in the opening verses? What has changed?
What seems to be the writer’s concern for his readers by the end of 2:4?

elements.text: 1:1–3 — God has spoken his final word in his Son

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>After God spoke long ago in various portions and in various ways to our ancestors through the prophets, <sup>2</sup>in these last days he has spoken to us in a Son, whom he appointed heir of all things, and through whom he created the world. <sup>3</sup>The Son is the radiance of his glory and the representation of his essence, and he sustains all things by his powerful word, and so when he had accomplished cleansing for sins, he sat down at the right hand of the Majesty on high.

elements.question: How does the writer summarise the whole of the Old Testament in v1? What does this tell us about the kind of God we are dealing with?
elements.sampleAnswer: The writer compresses the entire Old Testament into a single sentence: ‘After God spoke long ago in various portions and in various ways to our ancestors through the prophets.’ Two phrases do the heavy lifting. ‘Various portions’ — God’s revelation was not given all at once but in instalments across centuries: individual prophecies, the law, the psalms, visions, symbols, the temple system. ‘Various ways’ — the means were as varied as the content: direct speech, dreams, enacted signs, poetry, narrative. What this tells us about God is important: he is a communicating God. He has not been silent or hidden. Throughout Israel’s history he was persistently, patiently revealing himself. The OT is not a collection of human religious writings; it is God speaking. That framing raises the stakes enormously for what follows: if God was truly speaking in all of that, how much more seriously must we take what he says next.
elements.questionHint: Notice the two phrases ‘various portions’ and ‘various ways’ — what do they together suggest about the character of what came before Christ?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: What are some of the ‘various ways’ God spoke in the OT — can you think of examples? What do they have in common despite their variety?

elements.question: What has changed? What is different about how God has spoken ‘in these last days’?
elements.sampleAnswer: The contrast is sharp: before, God spoke through the prophets; now, he has spoken in a Son. The change is not just a matter of messenger — it is a change in the kind of communication. The prophets were servants who carried God’s word; the Son is himself the word, the heir of all things, the agent of creation. The phrase ‘in these last days’ is also significant and easily misread. It does not mean ‘the final few years before the end of history.’ In New Testament usage, ‘the last days’ refers to the whole era inaugurated by Christ’s coming — the period between his first and second comings. We are in the last days now. This means there is no further revelation of the same order to come. Christ is not one more instalment in a continuing series; he is the climax and conclusion of the whole series. The change is from preparation to fulfilment, from partial to final, from the servants who pointed forward to the Son who is himself the goal.
elements.questionHint: The phrase ‘in these last days’ is easy to misread as meaning ‘in the very near future.’ What does the New Testament actually mean by it — and why does it matter?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: If ‘these last days’ began with Christ’s coming, what does that mean for how we should understand our own moment in history?

elements.question: Count the descriptions of the Son in vv2–3. What picture do they build together? What strikes you most?
elements.sampleAnswer: The writer packs seven descriptions into two verses, and the accumulation is breathtaking. The Son is: (1) appointed heir of all things — everything that belongs to God belongs to him; (2) the agent through whom God created the world — the universe came into being through him; (3) the radiance of God’s glory — not a reflection of God but the outshining of God’s own being; (4) the exact representation of God’s essence — the precise imprint, as a seal on wax, of who God is; (5) the one who sustains all things by his powerful word — creation continues to exist because he holds it together; (6) the one who accomplished cleansing for sins — the priestly work; (7) now seated at the right hand of the Majesty on high — the kingly work complete, enthroned in the place of supreme authority. Taken together these descriptions are staggering: the one who became a human being and died for sin is the creator and sustainer of the universe, the very outshining of God’s own glory. The weight of what follows in the letter — the priesthood, the sacrifice, the new covenant — depends entirely on this identity.
elements.questionHint: Try listing the seven descriptions one by one. Which ones surprised you? Which feel most important for understanding what follows in the letter?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: The descriptions of the Son in vv2–3 cover the roles of prophet (revealing God), priest (purifying sins), and king (seated at God’s right hand). How does seeing all three in one person change how you think about Jesus?

elements.question: V3 says the Son ‘sat down at the right hand of the Majesty on high.’ Why is the posture of sitting significant? What does it tell us about his work?
elements.sampleAnswer: The detail of sitting is easy to pass over but the writer returns to it repeatedly (8:1; 10:12; 12:2), which signals that it is theologically significant. In the Old Testament tabernacle and temple, the priests never sat down while on duty — there were no chairs in the sanctuary. This was not an oversight; the work was never finished. Every sacrifice had to be repeated. Every year the high priest entered the Most Holy Place again because last year’s offering had not been enough. The standing posture enacted an ongoing, incomplete ministry. When the writer says the Son ‘sat down,’ he is making a theological claim: his work is complete. The sacrifice has been offered once. There is nothing more to add, nothing more to repeat. He has taken his seat because the job is done. The seated Christ is not resting from inactivity but reigning after accomplishment.
elements.questionHint: Old Testament priests stood while they served because their work was never finished. What does it mean that the Son ‘sat down’?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: If the Son’s work is complete — ‘accomplished cleansing for sins’ and now seated — what does that mean for how we should think about our own standing before God?

elements.text: 1:4–14 — The Son is superior to the angels

elements.passageText: The Son is the radiance of his glory and the representation of his essence, and he sustains all things by his powerful word, and so when he had accomplished cleansing for sins, he sat down at the right hand of the Majesty on high. 4Thus he became so far superior to the angels as he has inherited a name superior to theirs.

elements.question: Why do you think the writer moves from Christ’s supremacy over the prophets to his supremacy over the angels? Why might this comparison matter to his first readers?
elements.sampleAnswer: This question may feel slightly puzzling to modern readers, because very few people today are tempted to elevate angels above Christ. But for the letter’s original Jewish-Christian readers, the comparison was pointed for at least two reasons. First, Jewish tradition held that the law of Moses had been given through the mediation of angels (see Acts 7:53; Galatians 3:19). So to demonstrate that Christ is superior to the angels was, indirectly, to demonstrate that the new covenant he mediates is superior to the Mosaic law. Second, there are hints that some of the original readers may have had an exaggerated veneration of angels. The writer’s point is that the one they might be tempted to venerate is himself commanded to worship the Son. More broadly, the comparison sets up the letter’s central argument: Christ is not in a spectrum with other religious figures and mediators, however impressive. He is categorically different.
elements.questionHint: Why might Jewish Christians in the first century have been particularly impressed by angels? What role did angels play in relation to the law of Moses?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: What might be the equivalent for us today — what impressive-sounding things or figures might people be tempted to place alongside or above Christ?

elements.term: The Son and the angels: Hebrews 1:5–14
elements.question: The writer quotes seven Old Testament passages in a row to build his case that the Son is categorically superior to the angels. Work through each one below. For each passage, note: what does it tell us about the Son? And what does it tell us about the angels?

elements.passageText: <sup>5a</sup>For to which of the angels did God ever say, "You are my Son! Today I have fathered you"?

elements.question: Hebrews 1:5a quotes Psalm 2:7. What does it tell us about the Son? What does it imply about the angels?
elements.sampleAnswer: Psalm 2 is a royal psalm addressed to God’s anointed king. The declaration ‘You are my Son! Today I have fathered you’ is the public recognition of the Messiah’s unique status as God’s Son — not a statement that the Son came into existence at this moment, but that his identity as Son is proclaimed and enthroned. The implication for the angels is direct: God has never said this to any angel. No angel has ever been addressed as ‘my Son.’ The Son’s relationship to the Father is in a different category entirely from the relationship the angels have with God.
elements.questionHint: God has never addressed any angel as ‘my Son.’ What does that distinction between the Son and the angels imply?

elements.passageText: <sup>5b</sup>And in another place, "I will be his father and he will be my son"?

elements.question: Hebrews 1:5b quotes 2 Samuel 7:14, God’s promise to David about his son. How does the writer apply this to Christ? What does this tell us about the relationship between the Old Testament and Jesus?
elements.sampleAnswer: In 2 Samuel 7, God promises David that his dynasty will endure and that he will be a father to David’s son. The promise finds its ultimate fulfilment in Christ, the son of David who is also the eternal Son of God. The writer’s application of this verse to Jesus is not a creative reinterpretation; it is the recognition that the OT promise was always pointing beyond Solomon to something greater. This tells us something important about how the whole letter works: the writer reads the OT as a story that was always heading toward Christ. The promises made to David’s line, the priesthood of Melchizedek, the tabernacle — all of it was pointing forward. Jesus is not a new addition to the story; he is the one the story was always about.
elements.questionHint: The original promise in 2 Samuel 7 was to David about his royal line. How does applying it to Jesus change or deepen its meaning?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: If the Old Testament promises about David’s line find their fulfilment in Christ, what does that tell us about how we should read the Old Testament?

elements.passageText: <sup>6</sup>But when he again brings his firstborn into the world, he says, "Let all the angels of God worship him!"

elements.question: Hebrews 1:6 quotes Deuteronomy 32:43. What is striking about the relationship between the Son and the angels here?
elements.sampleAnswer: The striking thing is the direction of worship: the angels are commanded to worship the Son. If any of the original readers had been tempted to venerate the angels, this reversal is pointed: the beings they were tempted to revere are themselves on their knees before the Son. The one who is worshipped is always greater than those who worship. By showing that the angels worship the Son, the writer places the Son in an entirely different category — not the greatest among a spectrum of heavenly beings but the one to whom all heavenly beings bow.
elements.questionHint: What does the direction of worship — angels worshipping the Son — tell us about the relative standing of the Son and the angels?

elements.passageText: <sup>7</sup>And he says of the angels, "He makes his angels spirits and his ministers a flame of fire."

elements.question: Hebrews 1:7 quotes Psalm 104:4 about the angels. What does this tell us about what the angels are — and what they are not?
elements.sampleAnswer: The angels are described as spirits and ministers — wind and fire at God’s disposal. The imagery is of powerful, dynamic servants: impressive, perhaps awe-inspiring, but fundamentally functional and creaturely. They are not independent authorities; they are instruments in the hands of God. What they are not is what the Son is: they are not called Son, they are not worshipped, they do not have thrones, they did not create the world. The contrast between this verse and what follows (about the Son’s eternal throne) is sharp and deliberate. The angels are wind and fire; the Son is the eternal king.
elements.questionHint: The word ‘ministers’ or ‘servants’ is key here. What does it tell us about the angels’ role compared to the Son’s role?

elements.passageText: <sup>8</sup>But of the Son he says, "Your throne, O God, is forever and ever, and a righteous scepter is the scepter of your kingdom. <sup>9</sup>You have loved righteousness and hated lawlessness. So God, your God, has anointed you over your companions with the oil of rejoicing."

elements.question: In Hebrews 1:8–9, God addresses the Son directly as ‘O God’ and speaks of his eternal throne. What is the writer claiming here about who the Son is?
elements.sampleAnswer: This is one of the most direct affirmations of the Son’s deity in the New Testament. God himself addresses the Son as ‘O God’ — the Son is not merely a divine representative or a supremely exalted creature; he is addressed with the same title as God the Father. The throne is ‘forever and ever’ — not a temporary reign but an eternal one. The sceptre is righteous — his rule is morally perfect. Psalm 45 was originally a wedding hymn for a human king, but the writer applies it to Christ without hesitation, because Christ is the king the psalm was always ultimately about. The implication: what the Son is — eternally enthroned, morally perfect, addressed as God — no angel has ever been or could ever be.
elements.questionHint: God addresses the Son as ‘O God’ in v8. What does it mean that God himself uses this title for the Son?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: Psalm 45 was a wedding song for an Israelite king. What does it mean that the writer applies it to Jesus — and what does that tell us about how to read the Psalms?

elements.passageText: <sup>10</sup>"You founded the earth in the beginning, Lord, and the heavens are the works of your hands. <sup>11</sup>They will perish, but you continue. And they will all grow old like a garment, <sup>12</sup>and like a robe you will fold them up and like a garment they will be changed, but you are the same and your years will never run out."

elements.question: Hebrews 1:10–12 quotes Psalm 102, addressed in the original to God (the LORD). The writer applies it to the Son. What does this tell us about the Son’s relationship to creation — and to the angels?
elements.sampleAnswer: The application of Psalm 102 to the Son is striking because in the original psalm these words are addressed to YHWH, the LORD God of Israel. The writer applies them to the Son without explanation or apology, treating it as the natural reading. The content of the quotation tells us that the Son is the eternal Creator: he founded the earth and made the heavens. The universe will wear out and be changed, but he remains the same — his years will never run out. The implication for the angels is profound: the angels are part of creation. The Son is the one who made them. They are creatures; he is the Creator. No creature, however magnificent, can be placed in the same category as the one through whom all things were made.
elements.questionHint: Psalm 102 was originally addressed to God (YHWH). What does it mean that the writer simply applies it to the Son — without any explanation or qualification?

elements.passageText: <sup>13</sup>But to which of the angels has he ever said, "Sit at my right hand until I make your enemies a footstool for your feet"? <sup>14</sup>Are they not all ministering spirits, sent out to serve those who will inherit salvation?

elements.question: The section closes with Psalm 110:1 — the most quoted Old Testament verse in the New Testament. What is the final contrast the writer draws between the Son and the angels?
elements.sampleAnswer: The final contrast is one of position and authority. The Son is invited to ‘sit at my right hand’ — the place of supreme honour and power at God’s side — until his enemies are made his footstool. No angel has ever been offered that seat. The angels, by contrast, are described as ‘ministering spirits, sent out to serve those who will inherit salvation’ (v14). They are servants; the Son is the sovereign. They go where they are sent; the Son reigns. This verse also forms a bracket with v3 (which also references Psalm 110:1) — the whole catena of quotations is framed by the image of the Son enthroned at God’s right hand. That frame is the writer’s controlling image for everything he will say about Christ’s priesthood in what follows.
elements.questionHint: Notice that the question in v13 is rhetorical: ‘has he ever said this to an angel?’ What is the expected answer, and why does it matter?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: Psalm 110:1 is the most frequently quoted OT verse in the NT. Why do you think it was so important to the early Christians’ understanding of Jesus?

elements.question: Having worked through all seven quotations: what is the cumulative impression the writer wants us to have of Jesus? Is he the greatest among the angels, or something else entirely?
elements.sampleAnswer: The cumulative impression is carefully constructed to rule out a particular misunderstanding: the Son is not the greatest member of a spectrum that includes the angels. He is categorically different. He is the Son; they are servants. He is worshipped by them; they are not worshipped. He is the eternal Creator; they are creatures he made. He reigns at God’s right hand; they are sent out to serve. The seven quotations build this case from the OT itself — which means the writer is not arguing that Christianity has introduced a new claim about Jesus. He is showing that the scriptures the original readers already revered bear witness to Christ’s absolute supremacy. The cumulative effect is not subtle: there is no comparison to be made. The Son is in a different category altogether.
elements.questionHint: Does the writer argue that the Son is greater than the angels by degree, or that he is different in kind? What difference does that distinction make?

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>Therefore we must pay closer attention to what we have heard, so that we do not drift away. <sup>2</sup>For if the message spoken through angels proved to be so firm that every violation and disobedience received its just penalty, <sup>3</sup>how will we escape if we neglect such a great salvation? It was first communicated through the Lord and was confirmed to us by those who heard him, <sup>4</sup>while God confirmed their witness with signs and wonders and various miracles and gifts of the Holy Spirit distributed according to his will.

elements.question: What warning does the writer give in 2:1–4? What is his logic — why does the greatness of Christ make the danger of drifting so serious?
elements.sampleAnswer: The warning is against drift: ‘we must pay closer attention to what we have heard, so that we do not drift away.’ The image of drifting is important: it is not the dramatic departure of someone who storms out, but the slow, almost imperceptible movement of a vessel that has lost its mooring — carried away by current and wind without anyone noticing until it is too late. The logic is from lesser to greater. The message spoken through angels — the Mosaic law — was real divine speech, and those who violated it faced real consequences: every violation and disobedience received its just penalty. But what has now been spoken is incomparably greater: a salvation announced by the Lord himself, confirmed by eyewitnesses, attested by signs and wonders and the Holy Spirit. If ignoring the lesser brought serious consequences, what will happen to those who neglect the greater? The question is rhetorical: ‘how will we escape?’ The expected answer is: we will not. The greatness of Christ, established in chapter 1, is precisely what makes ignoring him so dangerous. This is not a stern rule; it is the logic of proportion.
elements.questionHint: The word ‘drift’ suggests a gradual, almost unnoticed process rather than a sudden decision. What kinds of things cause people to drift from Christ?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: The writer says the salvation was ‘first communicated through the Lord and confirmed to us by those who heard him.’ Why does the chain of testimony matter — and what does this tell us about the role of Scripture and apostolic witness?

elements.question: The writer describes this as ‘such a great salvation.’ Given everything established in 1:1–4 about who the Son is, what makes this salvation so great?
elements.sampleAnswer: The greatness of the salvation is the greatness of the Saviour. The writer has just established that the Son is the radiance of God’s glory, the exact representation of his essence, the creator and sustainer of the universe, the one seated at God’s right hand, the one whom angels worship. A salvation accomplished by this person is not a minor religious benefit. It is the intervention of the Creator of all things on behalf of his creatures. It is the outshining of God’s own glory taking on flesh and dealing with sin. To neglect it is therefore not like declining an invitation to a good event; it is like ignoring a message from the one who made you and holds your existence in being — a message that cost him everything to send.
elements.questionHint: The phrase ‘such a great salvation’ gets its weight from chapter 1. What specifically makes this salvation great, in light of who the Son has been shown to be?

elements.question: Which of the seven descriptions of the Son in 1:2–3 is most striking to you personally? What difference does it make to your daily life that Jesus is who this passage says he is?
elements.sampleAnswer: There is no single right answer here — the question is designed to move from exegesis to personal application. Some people will be most struck by ‘the radiance of his glory’ — the idea that in encountering Jesus, they are encountering the outshining of God’s own being. Others may find the most transformative idea in the ‘exact representation of his essence’ — if you want to know what God is like, you look at Jesus. Still others may be most moved by ‘accomplished cleansing for sins and sat down’ — the finality and completeness of what he has done. The pastoral goal is to help readers move from abstract admiration to concrete impact: if this is who Jesus is, what changes? How does it affect how I pray, how I approach God, how I think about my sin, how I face difficulty?
elements.questionHint: Try to be specific rather than general. Which description, and what concrete difference does it make — in prayer, in how you think about your sin, in how you face a particular difficulty?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: The writer says Christ ‘accomplished cleansing for sins and sat down.’ If the work is complete and he is seated, what does that mean for how you relate to God today?

elements.question: The writer warns against ‘drifting.’ In what ways are you personally tempted to drift? What might the antidote to drift look like for you, in light of this passage?
elements.sampleAnswer: Drifting rarely looks like a decision. It looks like neglect — letting prayer become irregular, letting Scripture become unfamiliar, letting other things gradually fill the space that Christ once occupied. The temptations vary by person: busyness, disappointment, the slow erosion of difficulty, the pull of something that feels more immediately satisfying than the invisible realities of faith. The antidote the passage suggests is not willpower but attention: ‘we must pay closer attention to what we have heard.’ The remedy to drift is not trying harder but fixing attention more firmly on Christ — on who he is (chapter 1) and what he has done. Practically, this might mean deliberate habits of returning to Scripture, worshipping with others, praying honestly, and keeping company with people who will help us keep our eyes on Jesus rather than on the currents pulling us away.
elements.questionHint: Drifting is gradual and often unnoticed. What are the small things that, if you are honest, have been loosening your grip on Christ? What would ‘paying closer attention’ look like in practice?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: How does knowing that Jesus is the final word — that there is nothing greater to come — change how you respond to things that seem to offer more than Christ?

elements.question: The writer shows from the Old Testament itself that Christ is supreme. How does this session change the way you read the Old Testament — or how you might approach it in future?
elements.sampleAnswer: For many readers, the Old Testament feels disconnected from the New — a different world, a different God, useful for stories and moral principles but not obviously about Jesus. This session should begin to shift that. The writer of Hebrews reads Psalm 2, 2 Samuel 7, Deuteronomy 32, Psalm 45, Psalm 102, and Psalm 110 — all as testimony to Christ. He is not forcing a reading onto these texts; he is showing that the texts themselves were always pointing forward. The key principle: the New Testament is the authoritative interpreter of the Old. If the New Testament tells us that a passage is about Jesus, then that passage is about Jesus. Reading the OT with that lens does not make it less interesting; it makes it immeasurably richer, because every page becomes part of the same story, heading toward the same person.
elements.questionHint: Before this session, how did you typically approach the Old Testament? Has anything about that changed? What would it look like to read it looking for Christ?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: Pick one of the seven Old Testament passages quoted in this chapter and look it up in its original context. What do you notice about how the writer of Hebrews has used it?

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Hebrews
studyOnboardingSlides.body:
A twelve-session Bible study on the letter to the Hebrews.

Work through each session at your own pace, or use it with a group.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Read, reflect, write
studyOnboardingSlides.body: Each session has Bible questions tied to specific passages, and personal reflection questions at the end. Tap ⓘ beside any Bible reference to read the passage without leaving the app.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Saved on your device
studyOnboardingSlides.body: Your answers are saved automatically as you type, and stored on this device. Use the Share button at the bottom of each session to send your answers to a group leader or mentor.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Adjust to your preference
studyOnboardingSlides.body: If you need bigger text go straight to settings now
studyOnboardingSlides.bodyAfter: Tap the ⚙ icon in the top corner to open Settings any time to change text size or switch to dark mode. Tap ☰ (contents) to jump to any session.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: How to use this app
studyOnboardingSlides.body: If you want a full guide to getting the most from this app and this study — whether on your own or in a group — you can tap the button below.

action.label: Go to the 'How to use' page →

studyOnboardingSlides.bodyAfter: You can also find it any time via the ? icon in the top navigation bar.

blocks.title: People tempted to drift
blocks.body: The letter to the Hebrews was written for people who were in danger of letting go — of quietly stepping back from Christ, of finding the cost of following him too high, of being drawn back toward something that felt more familiar or more manageable. If you have ever felt the pull of drift — a gradual loosening of grip on Christ rather than a dramatic departure — this letter was written for you. Its message is urgent and pastoral: do not neglect so great a salvation.

blocks.title: People who want to understand the Old Testament
blocks.body: Hebrews quotes or alludes to the Old Testament more densely than almost any other New Testament letter. It ranges across the Psalms, the Pentateuch, the prophets, and the wisdom literature — not as background decoration but as the very substance of its argument. Working through this study carefully will transform how you read the OT, because the writer shows us, again and again, that the whole OT is about Jesus.

blocks.title: People who need to be reminded that Jesus is enough
blocks.body: The letter makes one enormous claim and presses it from every angle: Jesus is better. Better than the prophets, the angels, Moses, the Levitical priesthood, the old covenant, and all its sacrifices. If you are someone who has been looking for something to add to Christ — a spiritual experience, a tradition, a system of practice — or who has been tempted to treat something else as the final word, this letter will call you back.

blocks.title: On your own
blocks.body: This study works well as a personal study. Each session begins with a prompt to read the relevant passage — take that seriously, since the questions are designed to reward close attention to the writer's actual words. Write your answers as you go, and give the reflection questions at the end real time. Hebrews rewards slow, attentive reading more than speed. The argument is cumulative, so earlier sessions lay foundations that later sessions build on.

blocks.title: In a group
blocks.body: The study works well in a small group setting. The most rewarding approach is usually for each person to work through the session individually first, writing their answers, and then for the group to come together to compare what they found. The themes of Christ's supremacy, the danger of drifting, and the call to perseverance generate rich discussion — and because the letter was written to a community of believers, reading it together has a particular fittingness.

blocks.title: Read the whole letter first
blocks.body: Hebrews is long enough that reading it in one sitting is a commitment — but it is a worthwhile one. Reading or listening to the whole letter before beginning the study gives you a feel for its tone, its urgency, and the way its argument builds. You will notice recurring themes — the warning against drift, the superiority of Christ, the call to hold firm — that give each individual session its larger context.

blocks.title: Working with a leader or mentor
blocks.body:
If someone has recommended this study to you — a pastor, a small group leader, or a trusted friend — that relationship matters. Hebrews raises personal questions about assurance, perseverance, and what genuine faith looks like under pressure. Having someone to think those questions through with can make the difference between the letter producing its intended encouragement and it becoming a source of unnecessary anxiety.

At the end of each session, the Share button lets you send your written answers directly to your leader or mentor — via WhatsApp, email, or any other app on your phone. This gives them a window into your thinking before you meet, and makes for a much richer conversation.

blocks.title: Two kinds of questions
blocks.body: Each session has two kinds of questions. The Bible questions follow the passage closely — asking you to look carefully at what the writer actually says, in context. The reflection questions at the end turn the same material toward you personally, asking what the message means for your own faith and life. Both kinds matter: the Bible questions build the foundation; the reflection questions are where the letter does its pastoral work.

blocks.title: Keeping the writer's purpose in view
blocks.body: Some passages in Hebrews can feel alarming when read in isolation — especially the warning passages in chapters 3, 6, 10, and 12. As you work through the questions, keep the writer's pastoral purpose in mind: he is not trying to terrify his readers into paralysis, but to wake them up and send them running toward Christ rather than away from him. When a passage feels difficult, the first question to ask is: how does this fit with the strong encouragements that surround it?

blocks.title: The Old Testament throughout
blocks.body: Hebrews quotes the Old Testament on almost every page. A key interpretive principle the writer himself assumes: Scripture interprets Scripture, and the New Testament is the authoritative interpreter of the Old. If the New Testament tells us that an Old Testament passage is about Jesus, then that passage is about Jesus. Working with this principle will open up the OT quotations rather than making them feel like obstacles.

intro: These notes are written for leaders guiding a group through an expository study of Hebrews. They are a resource, not a script. Work through each passage yourself first — read it, sit with it, ask what the writer is most urgent about — and then use these notes to fill in gaps, handle hard questions, and keep the group on track.

Know what kind of letter you are leading people through. Hebrews is not primarily a theological essay, though it contains dense theology. It is a pastoral sermon — what the writer himself calls ‘a word of exhortation’ (13:22). Every argument about Christ’s superiority is in service of a pastoral aim: to stop these believers from drifting away from Jesus. Keep that urgency in view as you lead. The theology is not an end in itself; it is a foundation for perseverance.

The central argument is cumulative. The writer builds his case layer by layer: Christ is greater than the prophets, than the angels, than Moses, than the Levitical priesthood, than the old covenant itself. Help your group follow the logic rather than treating each session as a standalone. The earlier sessions provide the foundation on which the later ones rest. It is worth beginning each session with a brief recap of where you have come from.

The Old Testament is everywhere. Hebrews quotes or alludes to the Old Testament more densely than almost any other New Testament letter. This is not incidental — the writer’s whole argument depends on the OT. A key interpretive principle worth establishing early: Scripture interprets Scripture, and in particular, the New Testament is the authoritative interpreter of the Old. If the New Testament tells us that an Old Testament passage is about Jesus, then that passage is about Jesus. Help your group grow in their ability to read the OT through that lens.

The warnings are serious and should not be softened. Hebrews contains some of the most sobering warnings in the New Testament (2:1–4; 3:7–19; 6:4–8; 10:26–31; 12:25–29). They are addressed to people who appear to be genuine members of the Christian community. Resist the temptation to explain them away. At the same time, each warning is embedded in a context of strong encouragement — the writer is not trying to terrify his readers into paralysis, but to wake them up and send them running toward Christ, not away from him. Hold both together.

The Background section in each session’s notes is for you, not necessarily for the group. It gives you the exegetical and historical grounding to handle unexpected questions with confidence. You do not need to deliver it as a lecture. The same applies to the Exegetical Notes — they are a resource, not a presentation.

Watch for the person who is overwhelmed by Hebrews’ OT density. Some in your group may feel lost when the writer cites Psalm 95 or Melchizedek or the tabernacle layout. A useful reframe: the point of the OT material is always to show something about Jesus. Ask the group, ‘What does this tell us about Christ?’ That question will usually cut through the unfamiliarity.

background.body:
Authorship and audience. Hebrews does not name its author. Paul, Apollos, Priscilla, Barnabas, and Luke have all been proposed, and all remain uncertain — Origen’s verdict in the third century (‘only God truly knows’) is still the honest answer. What is clear is that the recipients were a community of Jewish Christians, almost certainly familiar with the Septuagint (the Greek Old Testament), who were under pressure — social, perhaps legal — to abandon Christianity and return to Judaism. The letter is written to stop them doing that, and every argument is in service of that pastoral aim.


The Bible Project overview video (bibleproject.com/explore/video/hebrews/, approximately 8 minutes) provides an excellent introduction to the book’s structure and themes and is well worth watching with your group before the first session, or recommending as preparation.


‘In these last days’ (1:2). The phrase does not refer to a brief period immediately before the end of history, as it is often used in popular speech. In New Testament usage it refers to the whole era between Christ’s first and second comings — the period inaugurated by the incarnation and continuing until his return. We are in ‘the last days’ now. This matters because it means the readers (and we) are already living in the climactic phase of God’s purposes; there is no further revelation of the same order to wait for.


The seven descriptions of the Son (1:2–3). The writer packs an extraordinary Christology into two verses. The Son is: (1) appointed heir of all things; (2) the agent of creation; (3) the radiance of God’s glory; (4) the exact representation of God’s being; (5) the sustainer of all things; (6) the purifier of sins; (7) now seated at God’s right hand. Taken together these descriptions encompass the roles of prophet (revealing God), priest (purifying sins), and king (ruling at God’s right hand) — the three great mediatorial offices of the Old Testament, all fulfilled in Christ.


The catena of OT quotations (1:5–14). The writer strings together seven Old Testament texts to demonstrate Christ’s superiority to the angels. The quotations are from the Septuagint (LXX), which accounts for minor differences from standard English Old Testament translations. The logic of the catena is cumulative: (1) Christ is God’s Son, a title never given to angels (Ps 2:7; 2 Sam 7:14); (2) angels worship Christ, who is not worshipped (Deut 32:43 LXX); (3) angels are merely servants, Christ is the sovereign king (Ps 104:4; Ps 45:6–7); (4) Christ is the eternal creator, angels are creatures (Ps 102:25–27); (5) Christ reigns at God’s right hand, angels serve (Ps 110:1). The section is framed at beginning and end by the same image: Christ seated at God’s right hand (1:3 and 1:13), a reference to Psalm 110:1 which becomes the most quoted OT verse in the whole letter.


Why angels? Two reasons are likely. First, Jewish tradition held that the Mosaic law was mediated through angels (Acts 7:38, 53; Gal 3:19; cf. Josephus), so demonstrating Christ’s superiority to angels is equivalent to demonstrating the new covenant’s superiority to the old. Second, it is possible that some of the recipients had developed an exaggerated veneration of angels.

keyPoints.body:
The controlling claim of the whole letter is introduced here: God has spoken his final, definitive word in his Son. Everything that went before — prophets, angels, the whole apparatus of the old covenant — was genuine revelation, but it was partial, preparatory, and pointing forward. Christ is not another instalment in the series; he is the end and fulfilment of the series. He is what all of it was about.


The seven descriptions of the Son in 1:2–3 establish something crucial for the rest of the letter: the one who became our high priest and offered himself as sacrifice is not a creature of God but the very radiance of God’s glory and the exact representation of his being. This is not a capable human mediator doing his best; this is God himself, in human flesh, acting on our behalf. The weight of what follows — the priesthood, the sacrifice, the new covenant — depends entirely on this identity.


The superiority of Christ to the angels is demonstrated entirely from the Old Testament. This is deliberate: the writer is showing his readers that the supremacy of Christ is not a novelty invented by Christians, but the testimony of the scriptures they already revere. The OT itself tells us that the Son is in a different category from the angels.


The warning of 2:1–4 follows directly from the argument. The logic is simple and serious: if disobeying the word God spoke through angels brought real punishment, what will happen to those who ignore the word spoken by God’s own Son, confirmed by signs and wonders and the Holy Spirit? Drifting is not a neutral option. It is a response to God’s speech, and God takes it seriously.

pastorals.body:
The group may find the angel material remote or strange. Very few people today are tempted to venerate angels, so the writer’s focus can seem odd. Help the group see that the underlying issue is perennial: the temptation to treat something less than Christ as the final word, the ultimate authority, the thing that defines one’s relationship with God. For the original readers it was angels (and the Mosaic law they mediated). For your group it might be a tradition, a spiritual experience, a church culture, or a theological system. The question the passage asks is the same: Is Christ your final word, or is something else sitting alongside him or above him?


The warning in 2:1–4 is uncomfortable and needs careful handling. It is not addressed to unbelievers but to people who have already heard the gospel. The danger is not outright rejection of Christ but gradual, almost imperceptible drift — the slow loosening of grip rather than a dramatic departure. This will land personally for some in your group. The pastoral aim of the passage is not to produce anxiety but alertness. The writer’s antidote to drifting is not more willpower but a firmer grasp of who Christ is — which is exactly what 1:1–4 provides.

exegetical.body:
‘Radiance of God’s glory’ and ‘exact representation of his being’ (1:3). These two phrases work together. Apaugasma (radiance) can mean either the light that streams from a source (effulgence) or the reflection of a source (reflection) — most scholars favour ‘radiance’ here, indicating that the Son is not a reflection of God but the very outshining of God’s own glory. Charaktēr (exact representation) was used of the impression left by a seal on wax — a precise, faithful copy that shares the nature of the thing that made it. Taken together: the Son does not merely resemble God or represent him externally; he is the outshining of God’s own being and shares that being completely. This is as strong a statement of the Son’s deity as anywhere in the New Testament.


‘Sat down at the right hand of the Majesty in heaven’ (1:3). The seated posture is significant and the writer will return to it (8:1; 10:12; 12:2). In the Old Testament tabernacle and temple, priests never sat down — there were no chairs in the sanctuary — because their work was never finished. Christ sits because his work is complete. The posture is a theological statement about the finality and sufficiency of what he has done.


Deuteronomy 32:43 LXX (quoted in 1:6). The standard Hebrew text of Deut 32:43 does not include the line ‘Let all God’s angels worship him.’ This line is present in the Septuagint and in a Dead Sea Scrolls manuscript of Deuteronomy (4QDeutʲ). The NIV includes it as a footnote. The New Testament writers routinely quoted the LXX, the Bible of the Greek-speaking world. This does not undermine the argument — it shows the writer engaging with the text his readers knew.


‘Every violation and disobedience received its just punishment’ (2:2). The writer has in mind episodes like the judgment on those who approached Sinai unlawfully (Ex 19:12–13), Nadab and Abihu (Lev 10:1–3), and the wilderness generation who died without entering the land. These were not trivial incidents but serious divine responses to the word spoken through angels. The comparison that follows (‘how shall we escape if we ignore so great a salvation?’) is therefore an argument from the lesser to the greater.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the group getting stuck in the catena of OT quotations and losing the thread of the argument. The goal is not to unpack every individual quotation in detail but to see the cumulative point: from the OT’s own testimony, Christ is in a completely different category from the angels. If a quotation sparks a tangent about its original context, that’s worth a brief note — but bring the group back to the main argument.

studyQuestionsAndNotes.body:
*(The key themes of this session — the finality of Christ’s revelation, his deity, his superiority to angels, and the danger of drifting — are covered in the Background, Key Points, Pastoral Sensitivities, and Exegetical sections above. The notes below focus on guiding the group through the text question by question.)*

---

### Before reading: Introducing Hebrews

*(If using the Bible Project video, show it now — approximately 8 minutes. Hand out the Bible Project print-out before watching. This will cover authorship, audience, and the book’s basic structure. After the video, introduce the interpretive principle below before reading the passage.)*

Establish the following principle before diving into the text: we are working on the assumption that Scripture interprets Scripture, and in particular that the New Testament is the authoritative interpreter of the Old Testament. If the New Testament tells us that an Old Testament passage is about Jesus, then we take it that that passage is about Jesus. This will be important throughout the series.

---

### 1:1–3 — Christ is superior to the prophets

**Q1. How does the writer of Hebrews summarise the whole Old Testament in 1:1? What are the key elements of this summary?**

God is a speaking God who has been revealing himself throughout history. Before Christ came, he spoke through the prophets — and he did so in many different ways: direct prophecy, the temple system, the psalms, visions, symbols. Draw out the variety: ‘many times and in various ways’ is deliberate. God was not silent or inactive; he was continually communicating.

**Q2. What has changed?**

Now God has spoken through his Son. The contrast is not between revelation and silence, but between partial and final, between the servants who pointed forward and the Son who is himself the goal. This is not just a new instalment — it is the culmination. Help the group feel the weight of that: everything in the OT was building toward this.

**Q3. What is meant by ‘in these last days’? What is the significance of this for us?**

Not ‘the last little bit just before the end,’ as popular usage often implies. In New Testament thought, the ‘last days’ is the period inaugurated by Christ’s coming — we are already in them. This matters: it means there is no further revelation of the same order to come. Christ is the final word. We are not waiting for something more; we are living in the era of fulfilment.

**Q4. What do we learn about Christ in 1:2–3? How does he reveal God the Father to us? How is this superior to what the prophets did?**

Work through the seven descriptions: heir of all things, agent of creation, radiance of God’s glory, exact representation of God’s being, sustainer of all things, purifier of sins, seated at God’s right hand. The prophets were servants and messengers — they could point to God but could not fully reveal him. Christ is different in kind, not just in degree: he *is* the radiance of God’s own glory. He does not merely tell us about God; he shows us God. Note that the descriptions encompass prophet (revealing God), priest (purifying sins), and king (seated at God’s right hand) — every mediatorial role the OT knew, fulfilled in one person.

---

### 1:4–14 — Christ is superior to the angels

*(Consider dividing into groups of 3–4 for the handout exercise, then feeding back together. The handout asks groups to read each quotation and note what it tells us about Jesus and/or about angels. The summary below gives you the key points for each.)*

**Hebrews 1:5a / Psalm 2:7 — and 1:5b / 2 Samuel 7:14**

Christ is the Son of God. Note that ‘You are my Son’ does not imply that the Son was not Son before the resurrection — it is about the public recognition and enthronement of his status, the fulfilment of the Davidic covenant promise. No angel has ever been addressed as God’s Son.

**Hebrews 1:6 / Deuteronomy 32:43 LXX**

Angels worship Christ. The one the original readers might have been tempted to revere above Christ is in fact commanded to bow before him. If asked about the LXX difference, see the Exegetical Notes above.

**Hebrews 1:7 / Psalm 104:4**

Angels are servants — wind and fire in God’s hands. Impressive, perhaps, but functional and creaturely. The contrast with what follows (the Son’s eternal throne) is sharp.

**Hebrews 1:8–9 / Psalm 45:6–7**

The Son has an everlasting throne and rules with righteousness. The psalm, originally a wedding song for a king, is applied by the writer to Christ without apology — because Christ is the king the psalm was always about.

**Hebrews 1:10–12 / Psalm 102:25–27**

Christ is the eternal creator. Psalm 102 is addressed to God (YHWH); the writer applies it to the Son. The angels are creatures; the Son is the one who made them.

**Hebrews 1:13 / Psalm 110:1**

Christ sits at God’s right hand. No angel has ever been offered that seat. This verse — the most quoted OT text in the NT — frames the whole catena: 1:3 and 1:13 both reference it, forming a bracket around the angel comparison.

**Summarising the section:** Draw the group together with this question: *What is the cumulative impression the writer wants us to have of Jesus after reading these verses?* He is not on a spectrum with angels, merely higher up the scale — he is categorically different. He is the Son, the creator, the king, the one whom angels serve and worship.

---

### 2:1–4 — Do not drift away from Jesus

**Q5. What warning does the writer give? What is the logic of the argument?**

The logic is from lesser to greater. The word spoken through angels (the Mosaic law) was real divine speech and those who violated it were really punished. The word spoken through God’s own Son is incomparably greater speech. Therefore to drift from it, to neglect it, carries incomparably greater consequences. Note the word ‘drift’ (v1) — it is a nautical image, the gradual movement of a vessel that loses its mooring without anyone noticing. This is not dramatic apostasy but slow, almost imperceptible loosening of grip.

**Q6. What is meant by ‘so great a salvation’? Why is the Gospel described this way?**

Draw on what has just been established in chapter 1: this is salvation accomplished and announced by God’s Son — the radiance of God’s own glory, the purifier of sins, the one seated at God’s right hand. That is the weight behind the phrase. The greatness of the salvation is the greatness of the Saviour.

---

### Application

**Q7. What is especially striking about Jesus’ identity in this passage? What difference should that make to our daily lives?**

Let the group name what has struck them. Push toward the practical: if Christ is the radiance of God’s glory, what does that mean for how we pray, how we read the Bible, how we think about our salvation? If he is the final word, how does that affect how we respond to those offering something ‘new’ beyond or alongside Christ?

**Q8. In what ways are we tempted to drift? What might we think of as being more impressive or more immediately satisfying than Jesus?**

This is worth spending time on. Drifting rarely looks like a decision to leave Christ behind; it looks like slow neglect — letting prayer slip, letting Scripture become unfamiliar, letting other things fill the space that Christ used to occupy. What are those things for your group? Be specific and honest.

**Q9. How does knowing that Jesus is the climax of God’s revelation throughout Scripture affect how we read the Old Testament? How does it affect how we respond to people offering something ‘new’?**

The OT is not a separate religious document — it is the long build-up to Christ. Reading it with that in mind changes everything. And if Christ is the final word, then any claim to revelation beyond or outside of Christ is, by definition, a step backwards — trading the reality for something lesser.

chapters.title: Deuteronomy 4:1–43: The Lord Alone is God

background.body:
**Position in the sermon.** Chapter 4 is the climax of Moses' first sermon (1:6–4:40). Having rehearsed the history of Israel's failure and God's faithfulness in chapters 1–3, Moses now draws out the theological and ethical implications. The chapter functions as a great appeal: given everything you have seen and heard, there is only one rational response — listen to the Lord and worship him alone.

**The incident at Beth Peor (4:3–4).** Moses opens with a reference to recent events at Beth Peor (Numbers 25), where Israel's catastrophic idolatry — worshipping Baal of Peor and engaging in sexual immorality with Moabite women — resulted in a plague that killed 24,000 people. This is not ancient history for the audience; it has just happened. The wound is fresh. Moses uses it to make his warning concrete: this is what happens when you abandon the Lord.

**The uniqueness of Israel's God.** A key argument in this chapter is the incomparability of Israel's position among the nations. Other nations have their gods, but no other god speaks clearly to his people, draws near to them, and gives them righteous law. The implication is that Israel's temptation to idolatry is not just sinful — it is irrational. Why would you trade the living God for an idol?

**The prohibition of images (4:15–20).** The reason Moses gives for not making an image is striking: at Horeb, the people heard God but saw no form. Any image would therefore be a fabrication — a human invention imposed on a God who has chosen to make himself known through his word, not through visual representation. This is not an arbitrary restriction; it flows from the nature of who God is and how he has revealed himself.

**Cities of refuge (4:41–43).** Moses establishes three cities of refuge east of the Jordan before proceeding to his second sermon. These are only touched on briefly here (the fuller treatment comes in ch 19), but they are worth noting as an example of the righteous law just described: a legal provision that values human life, ensures justice, and prevents the cycle of blood vengeance.

keyPoints.body:
**The big idea: the Lord alone is God and blesses his people with his word, so listen to him and worship him only.** This is the culminating appeal of Moses' first sermon. Everything in chapters 1–3 has been building to this: history is an argument for obedience.

**Obedience is not arbitrary — it is the path to life.** The reasons Moses gives for following the Lord's decrees are consistently practical and relational: life in the land, historical precedent, the witness to the nations, God's nearness. Obedience is not blind rule-following; it is a rational and loving response to who God is and what he has done.

**Active remembering is a discipline, not a feeling.** The chapter's threefold warning (vv9, 15, 23) is all about the danger of forgetting. Moses does not assume that the people will naturally remember — he commands them to watch themselves, teach their children, and actively rehearse what they have seen and heard. Memory is a spiritual practice.

**God's word, not God's image.** Israel is unique among ancient religions in having a God who speaks rather than a God who is seen. The prohibition of images is not prudishness about art; it is a statement about the nature of revelation. God has chosen to make himself known through his word. Trying to represent him visually is not devotion — it is a form of control, reducing the living God to something that can be managed.

**Even judgment is an act of love.** The warning about exile and scattering (vv25–28) is not God abandoning his people — it is Moses honestly telling them what will happen if they walk away. And even in that warning, there is hope (vv29–31): if they return to the Lord, he will receive them, because he is merciful and will not forget his covenant.

pastorals.body:
**The command to teach children (vv9–10).** This will land differently depending on who is in your group. Parents may feel convicted or overwhelmed. Those who grew up without Christian teaching may feel grief. Those who are not parents may feel this section doesn't apply to them. Help the group see that the responsibility to pass on the faith is broader than biological parenthood — it belongs to the whole community of God's people.

**The uniqueness of the Lord and religious pluralism.** The claim that no other nation has a god like the Lord — who draws near, who speaks, who gives righteous law — is an exclusive claim in a pluralistic world. Some in your group may be uncomfortable with it. Don't soften it, but do help the group understand what it is and isn't claiming. It is not a claim that other religions are worthless or that their adherents are beyond God's reach; it is a claim about the character and actions of the living God as revealed in Scripture.

**The promise of return after exile (vv29–31).** For anyone who has wandered from faith and returned — or who is in the process of returning — these verses are extraordinarily tender. God is not a God who locks the door behind those who leave. If that applies to anyone in your group, let this passage breathe.

exegetical.body:
**'Do not add to or subtract from' (4:2).** This is not a claim that the canon is closed at this point; it is a warning against the kind of theological drift that either adds human tradition as binding requirement or shaves off inconvenient commands. The principle recurs at the end of Deuteronomy (12:32) and in the New Testament (Revelation 22:18–19).

**The threefold warning structure (vv9, 15, 23).** Identifying this structure for the group helps them see that Moses is making a cumulative case, not simply repeating himself. Each warning adds a dimension: first, don't forget what you saw; second, don't make an image of what you didn't see; third, don't forget the covenant altogether.

**'For the Lord your God is a consuming fire, a jealous God' (4:24).** Jealousy in God is not the petty, insecure jealousy of human experience. It is the appropriate response of a faithful covenant partner to betrayal — the refusal to share what belongs uniquely to that relationship with someone or something else. The fire imagery connects to Horeb: the same fire from which God spoke now threatens those who turn away from his word.

**'He brought you out… to bring you in' (4:37–38).** The logic of redemption in Deuteronomy is always purposive: God saves *in order to*. The exodus was not an end in itself; it was the means to a destination. This forward-pointing shape of salvation is worth drawing out — it is the same shape as the gospel.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the discussion of idolatry becoming abstract too quickly. The group will readily agree that worshipping literal statues is wrong. The harder and more useful question is what functional idolatry looks like today — what things do we treat as ultimate, find our security in, or refuse to let God have? Push gently past the theoretical and into the personal.

studyQuestionsAndNotes.body:
*(The structure of the threefold warning, the theology of God's word vs God's image, and the pastoral dimensions of this chapter are covered in the sections above. The notes below guide the group through the passage question by question.)*

---

### Introductory exercise

One person reads the whole chapter aloud while the group looks out for (and marks, if they have a printout) all the direct commands. This is a useful orientation before discussion: the chapter is full of imperatives, and identifying them helps the group see the shape of Moses' appeal.

---

### 4:1–8 — The Lord Makes His People Unique, So Listen to Him

**Introductory task: Read Numbers 25 and summarise it.**

The key points are that Beth Peor was the site of catastrophic idolatry — the people worshipped Baal and engaged in sexual immorality with Moabite women, and a plague killed 24,000. That is the recent, raw context for Moses' warnings against idolatry in this chapter. Don't spend too long on Numbers 25, but make sure the group registers the gravity of what happened and why Moses refers to it.

**Q2. What reasons are given in this section for hearing, following, and observing the decrees of the Lord?**

- v1–2: so they may live and possess the land (this theme recurs throughout: 5:33; 8:1; 11:9; 16:20; 30:6, 16, culminating in 30:19–20)
- v3–4: historical example — disobedience at Beth Peor led to death; those who held fast to the Lord lived
- v5–8: obedience demonstrates to surrounding nations that they are God's people — there is a witness dimension that the New Testament picks up

**Q3. What makes the people of God unique in this section?**

The Lord is near to them as no other nation's god is near. The Lord has given them righteous laws like no other nation has. The Lord has made them wise and understanding in the sight of the nations. It is the word and nearness of the Lord that makes his people unique — not their size, power, or virtue.

---

### 4:9–14 — Remember How God Spoke

**Q4. What is the first part of the threefold warning?**

Be careful not to forget what they have seen. The danger is that those memories will 'fade from their heart' — not dramatic apostasy, but quiet, gradual slippage.

**Q5. What can they do to stop themselves from forgetting?**

Watch themselves carefully and teach what they have seen to their children and grandchildren. Remembering requires active, deliberate effort — it will not happen automatically.

**Q6. What in particular are they to remember? What did they see, and what did they hear? Why does this matter?**

At Horeb, they saw fire and cloud and darkness — but no form. They heard his word: the covenant, the Ten Commandments. The absence of any visible form is theologically decisive: making an image of God when you saw no form is not devotion, it is invention. They could not see God, and they should not try to represent him. He gave them his word, and that is what they must cling to.

---

### 4:15–22 — Be Careful Not to Slide into Idolatry

**Q7. What is the second warning? What danger are the people in?**

Warned not to become corrupt and make idols in any form — human, animal, bird, reptile, fish. The danger is being enticed to worship created things instead of the Creator. The comprehensive list of possible forms emphasises that no created thing is an adequate representation of God.

**Q8. How does Moses help them 'watch themselves carefully'?**

By reminding them of three things: (1) the Lord is sovereign and apportions things to the nations; (2) the Lord brought them out of Egypt — they belong to him by redemption; (3) even Moses himself was excluded from the land — no one is immune from the consequences of departing from God's word.

---

### 4:23–31 — Be Careful Not to Forget the Lord's Covenant; Even When You Do, He Will Remember

**Q9. What is the third part of the warning? What reason is given?**

Do not forget the Lord's covenant. The reason: 'the Lord your God is a consuming fire, a jealous God.' The same fire from which God spoke at Horeb will consume those who turn away. Jealousy here is covenant faithfulness — the appropriate response to betrayal, not petty insecurity.

**Q10. What does Moses warn will happen if they forget and turn to idolatry?**

They will be sent from the land and scattered among the nations — forced to worship idols made of wood and stone (v28), which is itself presented as part of the judgment, not just the consequence. Exile is the unmaking of everything the covenant had given them.

**Q11. What hope do we see in this section? What does it tell us about the Lord's character?**

If they repent and seek the Lord, he will hear them (vv29–31). He is merciful and will not abandon his people or forget his covenant. Even the announcement of judgment contains a door left open. This is the shape of grace throughout Scripture.

---

### 4:32–40 — There Is No God Like the Lord, So Obey His Word

**Q12. What has the Lord done that is utterly unlike anything other nations claim for their gods?**

He actually is the Creator. He has spoken clearly to his people from fire. He has taken one nation for himself from among all nations. He has done great and awesome deeds to save his people. He has given his word and been with them. He has chosen them and given them a land. The argument is cumulative and rhetorical: has any other god done any of this? No. Then worship him.

**Q13. What is Moses' closing appeal (vv39–40)?**

Acknowledge the utter uniqueness of the Lord and obey his word — so that they and their children may be blessed with long life in the land. The appeal is both theological (acknowledge who he is) and practical (obey him). The two cannot be separated.

---

### 4:41–43 — Cities of Refuge

*(Don't spend long on this. The fuller treatment comes in ch 19. A brief note is sufficient.)*

The cities of refuge are a concrete example of the righteous laws just described: they value human life, distinguish between deliberate and accidental killing, ensure justice for the accused, and prevent the cycle of blood vengeance. They are law in the service of people, not law as an abstraction.

---

### Application Questions

*(Use the handout questions to guide small group discussion.)*

**What did the people do at Beth Peor and why? What was the Lord's response?**

They worshipped Baal and engaged in sexual immorality with Moabite women — following the gods of the nations around them at the point of greatest vulnerability (prosperity and proximity). The Lord's response was severe: plague and death. Idolatry is not a private matter; it has community consequences.

**How can we be tempted to drift from God's word?**

Encourage honest, specific answers. Drifting from Scripture often happens gradually and imperceptibly — not through dramatic rejection but through neglect, distraction, or the slow gravitational pull of other priorities.

**What idolatry is likely to tempt us as Christians? Read Hebrews 12:18–29. How can this help us avoid turning away from the Lord?**

Hebrews applies the Sinai imagery directly to Christian readers: we have come to a better mountain, with a better mediator, and we must not refuse him who speaks. The warning is the same as Deuteronomy's, intensified. The functional idols that compete with God today are typically good things elevated to ultimate things: security, status, family, comfort, control.

**What are we called to remember as Christians? How can we apply the warnings of Deuteronomy 4 to ourselves as New Testament believers?**

We are called to remember the cross and resurrection — the even greater act of redemption that grounds our obedience. The discipline of active remembering (rehearsing what God has done, teaching it to the next generation, returning to it in prayer and worship) is as necessary for us as it was for Israel.

**Deuteronomy 4 reminds us that the Lord is God and there is no other. What does it mean for us to reflect on the uniqueness of the Lord revealed in Christ?**

The series of rhetorical questions in vv32–34 can be reframed for a Christian audience: Has any other religion given a God who entered his own creation, died for his people's sin, and rose again? The answer shapes everything.

chapters.title: Deuteronomy 4:44–5:33: Walk in the Way the Lord Your God Has Commanded

background.body:
**The transition to Moses' second sermon.** Chapter 4:44–49 is an editorial introduction marking the beginning of Moses' second and longest sermon (5:1–28:68). This sermon is the heart of the book — it begins with the Ten Commandments and then unpacks their implications across a vast range of life situations. This session covers the framing of the Commandments: the context in which they were given, the character of the God who gave them, and the role of Moses as mediator.

**The context of the Ten Commandments.** The Commandments were given at Horeb (Sinai), in the aftermath of the exodus, to a people surrounded by pagan nations. They were not given to help people earn salvation; they were given to a people already redeemed, to show them how to live as the people of the God who had saved them. This order — redemption first, then law — is foundational and must not be reversed.

**The covenant at Horeb (5:3).** Moses makes the striking claim that the covenant was made 'not with our ancestors but with us, with all of us who are alive here today.' This is not a denial of historical continuity but an assertion of contemporaneity: each generation stands personally before God's word. The covenant is not an heirloom to be inherited; it is a living reality to be entered into afresh.

**Moses as mediator (5:23–31).** The people's terror at God's voice is not weakness — it is an accurate instinct. A holy God speaking directly to sinful people is genuinely dangerous. Their request for a mediator is not faithlessness; God commends it. Moses stands between God and the people, receives the word, and delivers it. This is a pattern of profound theological significance, pointing forward to the ultimate mediator.

**The purpose of the commands (5:32–33).** The section closes with a clear statement of purpose: obedience is not an end in itself. It is the path to life, to flourishing in the land God has given. The commands are not a burden; they are the shape of a blessed life.

keyPoints.body:
**The big idea: walk in the way the Lord your God has commanded you.** This is the summary of the entire second sermon before it has even begun. Everything that follows in chapters 5–28 is an unpacking of this one imperative.

**Redemption precedes law.** The Commandments begin: 'I am the Lord your God, who brought you out of Egypt, out of the land of slavery' (5:6). God introduces himself as Redeemer before he gives a single command. The law is given within a relationship already established by grace. This is not a new point for Christians, but it bears dwelling on — it is the structure of the gospel.

**The covenant is contemporary.** 'Not with our ancestors… but with us' (5:3). Moses insists that this covenant is not something that happened to a previous generation and has since been passed down. Each person standing before him is a direct party to it. That contemporaneity applies equally to those reading Deuteronomy today.

**The Ten Commandments as a vision of human flourishing.** Rather than treating the Commandments as a list of prohibitions to be avoided, help the group see them as a positive vision — of what a community shaped by love for God and neighbour actually looks like. Each commandment, approached from the positive direction, describes something beautiful.

**Moses as the pattern of the mediator.** The people cannot stand in the direct presence of God's holiness and survive. They need someone to stand in the gap, receive the word, and deliver it. Moses performs that function here, and explicitly: 'I stood between the Lord and you' (5:5). The New Testament identifies Jesus as the one who fulfils that role finally and completely (Hebrews 1:1–2; 1 Timothy 2:5).

pastorals.body:
**People have very different relationships with the Ten Commandments.** Some will have grown up with them as a kind of moral backdrop — vaguely familiar, not deeply considered. Some will have been taught them in a legalistic context and carry a burden of condemnation. Some will be encountering them seriously for the first time. Calibrate your introduction accordingly.

**The Sabbath commandment is a perennial flashpoint.** How Christians relate to the Sabbath is genuinely contested, and your group will likely have a range of views. The PDF notes suggest engaging with Hebrews 4 as a way of thinking about Sabbath rest in a Christian frame. Acknowledge the diversity of Christian practice and keep the focus on the theological principle — rest as trust, rest as reflection of God's own rhythm — rather than getting drawn into debates about Sunday trading.

**The terror of the people (5:25–27) is a pastoral gift.** It names something that people rarely say out loud: encountering the holy God is genuinely frightening. If anyone in your group is working through fear in relation to God — whether fear of judgment, fear of being truly known, or fear of what obedience might cost — this passage can open a conversation about why a mediator is not a weakness but a mercy.

exegetical.body:
**'Not with our ancestors but with us' (5:3).** This verse has puzzled readers: clearly the people present had ancestors who were at Horeb. The point is not chronological but existential — Moses is not allowing his audience to treat the covenant as something that applies to someone else. The same move is made in Christian preaching when the gospel is addressed to the present congregation, not just to the original recipients.

**The Sabbath rationale in Deuteronomy vs Exodus.** In Exodus 20:11, the Sabbath is grounded in creation (God rested on the seventh day). In Deuteronomy 5:15, it is grounded in redemption (remember that you were slaves in Egypt). Both grounds are given in different contexts. The Deuteronomy grounding is pastoral and social: the Sabbath is a reminder of freedom. Slaves do not get to rest; free people do. The command to let servants rest is therefore an extension of the exodus logic.

**'I stood between the Lord and you' (5:5).** The Hebrew verb translated 'stood between' (*'amad bên*) is the language of mediation and intercession. Moses is not merely a messenger; he is a buffer, a representative, one who bears the weight of the encounter so others can receive the word safely. This is the theological heartbeat of the passage.

**'Walk in all the way the Lord your God has commanded you' (5:33).** The singular 'way' (*haderekh*) is significant. Obedience in Deuteronomy is not a fragmented list of individual rules; it is a single path, a whole manner of life. The image of walking recurs throughout the book and is taken up extensively in the New Testament.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the group spending all its time on the content of individual commandments and missing the frame around them — why God gave them, to whom, and with what goal. The commandments are important, but this session's particular contribution is the *context* of the commandments: given by a Redeemer, received through a mediator, aimed at a flourishing life. Keep coming back to that frame.

studyQuestionsAndNotes.body:
